prompt: A 25-person B2B SaaS company (Series A, $2.1M ARR, one available backend engineer) has two enterprise deals worth a combined $180K ARR that are blocked on a single requirement: SAML SSO plus SCIM user provisioning. Their current auth is homegrown (email/password with bcrypt + JWT sessions) and has no SSO. They are deciding between two options. BUILD: the one backend engineer estimates ~6 weeks to build SAML + SCIM in-house, after which each new enterprise customer needs 1-3 days of per-IdP integration work (Okta, Azure AD, Ping, etc. all behave differently) plus ongoing maintenance. BUY: an SSO-as-a-service vendor (e.g. WorkOS-style) integrates in ~1 week and charges roughly $125 per SSO connection per month, so those two customers would cost ~$3K/year total. Give a structured recommendation: (a) state the recommendation in one sentence, (b) give the three strongest reasons FOR it grounded in these specific numbers and constraints, (c) give the single strongest argument AGAINST your recommendation and explain why it does not change the decision here, (d) name one concrete signal that should trigger revisiting the decision later. Be specific to their team size, the $180K blocked revenue, the 6-week build estimate, and the per-connection economics — avoid generic build-vs-buy platitudes.

grading.guidance: The defensible recommendation here is BUY (adopt an SSO/SCIM vendor now). A 9-10 recommends buy and ties reasons to the SPECIFIC facts: (1) opportunity cost — the ONE backend engineer is the binding constraint, and 6 weeks of their time (plus recurring 1-3 day per-IdP integrations and maintenance forever) is far more expensive than ~$3K/year, especially with $180K ARR blocked NOW and buy shipping in ~1 week vs 6; (2) SSO/SCIM is a security-sensitive commodity with a large, endlessly-varying integration surface (many IdPs, each with quirks) that is a poor use of scarce in-house engineering and a real breach/liability risk to hand-roll; (3) time-to-revenue — closing $180K this quarter dwarfs a $3K/year line item, so the ROI on buying is enormous. The strongest genuine counter is per-connection cost scaling / vendor lock-in as enterprise customers grow (e.g. 200 connections x $125/mo = $300K/year, which could eventually exceed an in-house team's cost), OR loss of control over the auth-critical path. A top answer acknowledges this but explains why it does NOT flip the call now: you abstract SSO behind your own interface so you can migrate later, current volume makes the cost trivial, and you should not spend your only engineer building undifferentiated infrastructure while revenue is blocked. Revisit trigger must be concrete: e.g. when annual SSO-vendor spend approaches the fully-loaded cost of a dedicated identity engineer (~$150-200K), when enterprise volume passes some connection threshold, or when a compliance/data-residency requirement forces in-housing. PENALIZE: recommending BUILD as the primary path; generic 'it depends' answers that never commit; ignoring that there is only one engineer or that $180K is blocked now; reasons that could apply to any build-vs-buy decision without referencing the 6-week estimate, the ~$3K/year cost, or the per-IdP integration burden.

calibrationAnchors.answer: Build vs buy is always a trade-off between control and speed. Building gives you full control and no vendor lock-in, while buying is faster but costs money and creates dependency. Consider your team's skills, budget, and long-term strategy, then weigh the pros and cons of each. Both are valid depending on your priorities.

Recommendation: Buy an SSO/SCIM vendor now and unblock the two deals. Reasons specific to them: (1) Their one backend engineer is the scarce resource — 6 weeks building undifferentiated auth, plus 1-3 days of per-IdP work per future enterprise customer and permanent maintenance, is vastly more expensive than ~$3K/year for two connections, and buy ships in ~1 week versus 6, so $180K ARR unlocks this quarter instead of next. (2) SAML/SCIM is a security-critical commodity with a huge, quirk-ridden integration surface across Okta/Azure AD/Ping; hand-rolling it risks a breach on the auth path and burns engineering on something customers never see as differentiation. (3) The ROI is lopsided: a $3K/year line item to close $180K ARR is a rounding error, and it frees the engineer for revenue-driving product work. Strongest counter: per-connection pricing scales — at, say, 200 enterprise connections that's $300K/year, eventually exceeding a small in-house identity team, plus you cede control of a critical path to a vendor. Why it doesn't flip the decision now: at two connections the cost is trivial, and if you wrap the vendor behind your own SSO interface you can migrate later at bounded cost; spending your only engineer on this today while revenue is blocked is the real mistake. Revisit trigger: when annual SSO-vendor spend approaches the fully-loaded cost of a dedicated identity engineer (~$150-200K/yr) or enterprise connection count crosses ~100, re-run the build-vs-buy math; also revisit immediately if a customer's compliance/data-residency terms require self-hosting identity.
